briefs.title: Council to Consider: Territorial Acknowledgement at Burnaby Meeting
briefs.summary: Burnaby City Council is set to include a Territorial Acknowledgement as a formal agenda item at its April 28, 2026 meeting. This is a ceremonial recognition of the Indigenous peoples on whose traditional lands Burnaby sits, and does not propose any new rules or requirements for businesses.

briefs.title: Council to Consider Burnaby Open Meeting Agenda — April 28, 2026
briefs.summary: Burnaby City Council is set to meet on April 28, 2026, to consider items on the open council meeting agenda. The specific proposals and bylaws up for discussion have not been detailed in the available agenda text, so business owners should review the full agenda directly to identify any items that may affect their operations.
briefs.action_required: Check the full Burnaby Council agenda for April 28, 2026 to spot any items affecting your business.

key_dates.label: Burnaby City Council open meeting — April 28, 2026

briefs.title: Council to Consider: North Road BIA Renewal Abandoned, PST on Services Referred to Staff
briefs.summary: At the April 14, 2026 Burnaby Council meeting, several items affecting local businesses came up. The proposed renewal of the North Road Business Improvement Area (BIA) bylaw — a special zone where businesses collectively fund local improvements — was abandoned after enough property owners signed petitions against it. Separately, a letter from the Canadian Federation of Independent Business urging Council to oppose a provincial expansion of the PST (Provincial Sales Tax) to professional services was referred to staff for further review and a future report back to Council.
briefs.action_required: Watch for the staff report on PST expansion — it could affect costs for your professional service providers.

briefs.title: Council to Consider Administrative Reports at April 28, 2026 Burnaby Meeting
briefs.summary: Burnaby City Council is set to review and vote on a series of administrative reports at its April 28, 2026 meeting. The details of each report have not been provided, so business owners should check the full agenda on the City of Burnaby's website to see if any proposals could affect their operations.
briefs.action_required: Check the full April 28, 2026 Burnaby Council agenda for details that may affect your business.

key_dates.label: Burnaby Council meeting — administrative reports considered

briefs.title: Proposed: Burnaby to Replace 60-Year-Old Zoning Bylaw with New Rules Effective July 1, 2026
briefs.summary: Council is set to vote on replacing Burnaby's Zoning Bylaw 1965 — the rulebook that controls what can be built and where — with a new Burnaby Zoning Bylaw 2026. If approved, the new bylaw would take effect July 1, 2026, and would change how building height, parking, green space, and tenant protections are regulated across the city, affecting property owners, developers, landlords, and businesses.
briefs.action_required: Check if your property's zoning district is changing — the new Official Zoning Map takes effect July 1, 2026.

key_dates.label: Burnaby Zoning Bylaw 2026 proposed effective date — new rules take effect
Burnaby Zoning Bylaw 2026 — deadline to apply for building permit under old rules (PPA transition)
Burnaby Zoning Bylaw 2026 — in-progress rezoning bylaws must be adopted by this date

briefs.industries: Construction & Development, Landlord & Property Mgmt, Tenant

briefs.title: Council to vote on $2M transfer to Burnaby Housing Authority for Year 2 operations
briefs.summary: Burnaby City Council is set to vote on releasing $2.0 million in Year 2 operating funding to the Burnaby Housing Authority Corporation (BHA) — a city-owned body created to build and manage affordable and market rental housing in Burnaby. If approved, the money would come from the City's Operating Housing Reserve and go toward running the BHA through its second year of operation.
briefs.action_required: Watch for the April 28, 2026 council decision if you work in Burnaby housing development or property management.

key_dates.label: Burnaby Council vote — BHA Year 2 funding approval

briefs.industries: Landlord & Property Mgmt, Construction & Development, Finance & Insurance

briefs.title: Council to vote on Burnaby's 2026 property tax rates, including a 2.9% increase
briefs.summary: Burnaby City Council is set to vote on the 2026 property tax rates for all property classes, including businesses. A proposed bylaw would set the general municipal tax rate 2.9% higher than last year, plus an additional 1.9% increase tied to growth infrastructure (funding for new development-related costs). Business and commercial properties (Class 6) would face a combined municipal mill rate of $6.39 per $1,000 of assessed value.
briefs.action_required: Check your property class and review the proposed rates — your 2026 tax bill will reflect these new amounts if approved.

key_dates.label: Burnaby Rates Bylaw 2026 — Council vote at open meeting
Burnaby 2026 property tax rates — statutory deadline to pass bylaw
Hastings BIA — first tax remittance (30%) deadline
Hastings BIA — second tax remittance (70%) deadline
MVRD regional tax — City remittance to Metro Vancouver deadline

briefs.industries: Landlord & Property Mgmt, Retail, Construction & Development

briefs.title: Council to vote on appointing a new Approving Officer for Burnaby subdivisions
briefs.summary: Burnaby Council is set to vote on appointing Mark Norton, Manager of Development, as an Approving Officer — the city official authorized to approve subdivision applications (requests to divide or re-divide land). This would add Norton to a team of three existing Approving Officers, helping ensure applications are processed without delays when staff are unavailable.
briefs.action_required: Watch for the council decision if you have a subdivision application pending in Burnaby.

key_dates.label: Burnaby Approving Officer appointment — council vote date

briefs.title: Council to vote on approving oversized retaining walls at 8012 11th Avenue, Burnaby
briefs.summary: Burnaby Council is set to vote on a Development Variance Permit (DVP) — a special approval that allows a property to bend a specific zoning rule — for 8012 11th Avenue. The permit would allow retaining walls on the east and west sides of the property to stand taller than the 1.2 m limit set by city rules. The walls were already built and were found to exceed the allowed height during a city inspection.
briefs.action_required: If you own or occupy a nearby property, attend the April 28, 2026 Council meeting to have your say.

key_dates.label: Burnaby DVP #26-01 retaining wall variance — Council vote

briefs.title: Council to vote on $4 million insurance contract renewals for Burnaby city assets
briefs.summary: Burnaby City Council is set to vote on approving $4,013,855 in insurance premiums covering 12 contracts for the city's property, liability, cybersecurity, and other insurance programs. The total is about $180,000 (4%) less than last year, with the biggest savings coming from a 15% drop in the main property insurance premium.
briefs.action_required: Watch the April 28, 2026 council meeting outcome — this affects city operating budgets across departments.

key_dates.label: Burnaby 2026 insurance programs renewal takes effect

briefs.title: Council to vote on: $4,000 for Heritage Commission members to attend Squamish conference
briefs.summary: Burnaby Council is set to vote on spending up to $4,000 to send two members of the City's Community Heritage Commission to the 2026 Heritage BC Conference in Squamish, BC (May 4–6, 2026). The funds would cover registration, hotel, meals, and travel — and the money is already set aside in the City's advisory bodies budget.
briefs.action_required: No action needed — this spending decision does not directly affect private businesses.

key_dates.label: 2026 Heritage BC Conference takes place in Squamish, BC

briefs.title: Council to vote on $1M+ networking software contract for Burnaby city systems
briefs.summary: Burnaby City Council is set to vote on awarding a contract worth roughly $1,047,545 (including taxes) to Insight Canada Inc. for Cisco DNA software licenses — the digital tools that keep the city's internet, Wi-Fi, payment systems, and file sharing running securely. The proposed three-year contract includes options to extend for up to two additional one-year terms.
briefs.action_required: Watch for the April 28, 2026 council decision if you supply IT products or services to the City of Burnaby.

key_dates.label: Burnaby Council vote — networking software contract award decision

briefs.title: Council to vote on: $1.96M furniture contract for five new Burnaby community facilities
briefs.summary: Burnaby Council is set to vote on awarding a contract worth roughly $1.96 million (plus a $196,260 contingency buffer) to Heritage Office Furnishings Ltd. to supply, deliver, and install furniture across five new city facilities opening over the next five years. The five locations include the James Cowan Theatre, Community Safety Building, Burnaby Lake Recreation Centre, Cameron Community Centre and Library, and Brentwood Community Centre.
briefs.action_required: Watch the April 28, 2026 council decision if you supply furniture or fit-out services — this contract has been awarded.

key_dates.label: Burnaby Council meeting — furniture contract award vote

briefs.title: Council to vote on $47,000 contract increase for Burnaby's external audit services
briefs.summary: Burnaby City Council is set to vote on approving a $47,000 increase to the existing contract with KPMG LLP for external audit services. The extra cost is needed because new public accounting rules — standards that govern how governments report their finances — were introduced after the original five-year contract was signed in 2021, requiring additional audit work that wasn't part of the original agreement.
briefs.action_required: Watch for the April 28, 2026 council decision if your business provides audit or financial services to the City.

key_dates.label: Burnaby external audit services contract expiry — KPMG LLP

briefs.title: Council to vote on $384,893 contract increase for Burnaby Lake Recreation Complex ground studies
briefs.summary: Burnaby Council is set to vote on expanding a contract with WSP Canada Inc. to cover extra geotechnical (soil and ground condition) consulting work at the Burnaby Lake Recreation Complex. Unexpected poor ground conditions discovered during construction have made additional engineering studies and design work necessary, pushing the proposed contract change to $384,893 plus a $73,500 contingency (backup fund) — bringing the total contract value to roughly $1.1 million.
briefs.action_required: Watch for the April 28, 2026 council decision if you work in construction or supply services to this project.

key_dates.label: Burnaby Council vote — Burnaby Lake Recreation Complex geotechnical contract change

briefs.title: Council to Consider Committee Reports at Burnaby's April 28, 2026 Meeting
briefs.summary: Burnaby City Council is set to review and vote on committee reports at its upcoming meeting on April 28, 2026. The body of the agenda item was not provided, so specific proposals, bylaws, or decisions within those reports are not yet known.
briefs.action_required: Check the full Burnaby Council agenda for April 28, 2026 to see if any committee reports affect your business.

key_dates.label: Burnaby City Council meeting — committee reports up for consideration

briefs.title: Council to vote on $215,000+ in Spring 2026 community grants for Burnaby nonprofits
briefs.summary: Burnaby City Council is set to vote on approving 31 community grants totalling over $215,000 for the Spring 2026 intake. The grants would support local nonprofits across five categories: recreational sports, operating costs, community initiatives, subsidized use of city facilities, and food security programs.
briefs.action_required: If your business sponsors or partners with any of these nonprofits, watch for the council vote outcome on April 28, 2026.

key_dates.label: Burnaby Spring 2026 Community Grants — Council vote date

briefs.industries: Food Service, Grocery & Food Retail, Professional Services

briefs.title: Proposed: Burnaby to raise recreation fees and launch Hotel Burnaby pricing
briefs.summary: Council is set to vote on mid-year changes to Burnaby's fee bylaw — a bylaw is a local law that sets official prices for city services. A proposed bylaw would update Parks, Recreation and Culture fees starting June 1 and September 1, 2026, add room rates for the new Hotel Burnaby, and lower the age for a free Seniors Lifetime Card from 90 to 80.
briefs.action_required: Check the updated fee schedules on the Burnaby city website after the April 28 council vote.

key_dates.label: Burnaby Consolidated Fees and Charges Bylaw — Schedule F1 (Hospitality/Hotel Burnaby) takes effect
Burnaby Consolidated Fees and Charges Bylaw — Schedule F3 (Recreation and Culture fees) takes effect

briefs.industries: Hotel & Accommodation, Food Service, Spa & Wellness

briefs.title: Proposed: Burnaby Tenant Protection Bylaw to Replace Existing Rental Displacement Policy
briefs.summary: Council is set to vote on a proposed Burnaby Tenant Protection Bylaw 2026 that would replace the current Tenant Assistance Policy and extend protections to renters displaced by redevelopment — even when no rezoning is required. The proposed bylaw would give the City new enforcement tools, including fines up to $500 and the ability to stop construction, to ensure developers provide required tenant benefits such as financial compensation, moving costs, and the right to return to a replacement unit at their old rent.
briefs.action_required: If you own or develop rental property in Burnaby, review the proposed bylaw before the July 1, 2026 effective date.

key_dates.label: Burnaby Tenant Protection Bylaw 2026 proposed effective date — takes effect
Burnaby Council vote on Tenant Protection Bylaw — public meeting

briefs.industries: Construction & Development, Landlord & Property Mgmt, Tenant

briefs.title: Council to vote on Southeast Bikeway Upgrade: new bike lanes, signals, and traffic changes
briefs.summary: Burnaby City Council is set to vote on a package of cycling improvements called the Southeast Bikeway Upgrade Project. If approved, the plan would add protected bike lanes, upgrade traffic signals, create multi-use pathways near schools, and change traffic flow on 12th Avenue — affecting roads between Edmonds Town Centre and the Big Bend area, including parts of North Fraser Way.
briefs.action_required: If your business is near the Southeast or Big Bend corridors, review the proposed changes before the April 28 vote.

key_dates.label: Burnaby Southeast Bikeway Upgrade — Council vote at City Council meeting

briefs.title: Council to Consider Safer Intersection Design for Burnaby Town Centre Streets
briefs.summary: Burnaby City Council is set to vote on updating the Town Centre Design Standards to include 'protected intersection geometry' — a road design that adds physical barriers, set-back crossings, and refuge islands to keep cyclists and pedestrians safer where streets cross. If endorsed, the new design rules would apply to newly built intersections on major streets in Brentwood, Metrotown, Lougheed, and Edmonds as redevelopment happens.
briefs.action_required: Watch the April 28, 2026 Council meeting if your business is near a Burnaby Town Centre redevelopment area.

key_dates.label: Burnaby Town Centre Design Standards amendment — Council vote

briefs.title: Council to Consider Burnaby Consent Agenda Items — April 28, 2026
briefs.summary: Burnaby City Council is set to vote on a package of items grouped under the Consent Agenda at its April 28, 2026 meeting. A Consent Agenda bundles routine or non-controversial items into a single vote — meaning all items can be approved at once unless a councillor pulls one out for separate discussion. The specific items have not been detailed in the provided agenda text, so business owners should review the full agenda to check for anything that may affect their operations.
briefs.action_required: Review the full Burnaby Council agenda for April 28, 2026 to check if any items affect your business.

key_dates.label: Burnaby Council Consent Agenda vote — April 28, 2026 meeting

briefs.title: Council to Consider: Brentwood Park Cannot Be Exempted from Provincial Housing Rules
briefs.summary: Council is set to receive a staff report explaining why Burnaby cannot exempt Brentwood Park from provincial small-scale multi-unit housing (SSMUH) rules — laws that require cities to allow triplexes, fourplexes, and small apartment buildings on most residential lots. Staff found no legal way to carve out just one neighbourhood, and warn that a city-wide building bylaw change to enforce old private land restrictions (called Restrictive Covenants) would slow down permits, raise costs, and create problems across Burnaby.
briefs.action_required: If you own property in Brentwood Park or plan to build, watch for Council's response at the April 28, 2026 meeting.

key_dates.label: Burnaby Council meeting — Brentwood Park SSMUH exemption report considered

briefs.title: Council to receive Burnaby building permit activity report for March 2026
briefs.summary: At its April 28, 2026 meeting, Burnaby City Council is set to receive a routine information report covering all building permits issued in Burnaby during March 1–31, 2026. No new rules or decisions are being made — Council will simply review the data on local construction activity. The report will be posted publicly on the City of Burnaby website after the meeting.
briefs.action_required: Check the City of Burnaby website after April 28 to view the full permit activity report.

briefs.title: Council to Consider Heritage Documentation for Forest Lawn Memorial Park Chapel Before Demolition
briefs.summary: Council is set to receive — for information only — two heritage documents about Forest Lawn Memorial Park at 3789 Royal Oak Avenue in Burnaby: an as-found report (a detailed record of the Chapel building before it is torn down) and a Statement of Significance (a document identifying what makes the site historically important). These were required as conditions of a rezoning application that proposes to demolish the Chapel and redevelop the funeral home facilities.
briefs.action_required: If you have a connection to Forest Lawn Memorial Park, review the heritage documents before the April 28, 2026 Council meeting.

key_dates.label: Burnaby Council meeting — heritage documents for Forest Lawn Chapel received for information

briefs.title: Council to Consider: Burnaby's 2025 Solid Waste and Recycling Annual Report
briefs.summary: Burnaby City Council is set to receive the 2025 Solid Waste and Recycling Annual Report for information at its April 28, 2026 meeting. The report shows the city collected 74,489 tonnes of waste and recyclables in 2025, hit a 61% diversion rate, and outlines upcoming plans like curbside flexible plastics pilots, more cleanup events, and expanded recycling outreach.
briefs.action_required: If your business generates construction waste or recyclables, review the planned program changes that may affect your operations.

briefs.title: Council to Consider: Burnaby's Update on Plant-Based Food Options at City Facilities
briefs.summary: Burnaby City Council is set to receive an update on food sustainability efforts at city-run cafes and restaurants. The report — up for consideration at the April 28, 2026 meeting — outlines steps staff have already taken to add plant-based, vegetarian, vegan, and dairy-free menu options at locations like Riverway and Mintara, while keeping services financially responsible and customer-focused.
briefs.action_required: If you supply food or services to Burnaby city facilities, monitor how plant-based menu demand may shift your contracts.
briefs.industries: Food Service, Grocery & Food Retail, Agriculture

briefs.title: Proposed: Burnaby Council to Consider Pedestrian Safety Upgrades Near Metrotown Parkade
briefs.summary: Council is set to receive a report on proposed safety improvements at the busy intersection of Central Boulevard and the Metropolis at Metrotown underground parkade entrance. The plan would add a 'pedestrian scramble' signal — where all cars stop and people can cross in any direction, including diagonally — plus upgraded curb ramps, better accessible signals, and removal of a right-turn island to reduce pedestrian exposure to traffic. Construction is targeted for Summer 2026.
briefs.action_required: If your business is near Metrotown, watch for construction notices — disruptions are expected Summer 2026.

key_dates.label: Central Boulevard & Parkade intersection construction completion target

briefs.title: Proposed: Burnaby Council to vote on adding hotel, restaurant uses and 362 units at Lougheed towers
briefs.summary: Council is set to vote on a proposed bylaw that would amend an already-approved rezoning for two high-rise towers at 3900 Grand Promenade and 6950 Austin Road. The changes would swap some planned office space for hotel and restaurant uses, add 362 more residential units, make both towers taller, and shift an affordable housing requirement to a nearby site called Carrigan.
briefs.action_required: Watch for the council vote — this could affect nearby commercial leasing, hotel, and food service opportunities.

key_dates.label: Burnaby Zoning Bylaw Amendment No. 7, 2026 (REZ #24-23) — council consideration date

briefs.industries: Construction & Development, Hotel & Accommodation, Food Service

briefs.title: Proposed: 12-Storey Mixed-Use Tower at 6535 Kingsway Up for Burnaby Council Approval
briefs.summary: Burnaby Council is set to vote on a proposed zoning bylaw (Bylaw No. 14809) that would rezone 6535 Kingsway to allow a 12-storey building with 290 homes and ground-floor commercial space facing Kingsway, plus underground parking. If approved, the development — called the Intracorp Kingsway Mixed-Use Development — would bring new retail or commercial tenants and residents to the Edmonds area.
briefs.action_required: Watch for the council vote — approval could bring new commercial space and foot traffic to the Kingsway corridor.
briefs.industries: Construction & Development, Retail, Landlord & Property Mgmt

briefs.title: Proposed: Burnaby Rezoning at 2757 Norland Avenue to Allow Five-Storey Self-Storage Building
briefs.summary: Council is set to vote on a proposed zoning bylaw (Bylaw No. 14810) that would rezone the property at 2757 Norland Avenue in Burnaby. If approved, the rezoning would allow a developer to build a five-storey enclosed self-storage facility on the site, designed by Architecture Panel, Inc. under the Public Storage brand.
briefs.action_required: Watch for the council vote — this could affect nearby businesses or your storage and logistics needs.
briefs.industries: Construction & Development, Landlord & Property Mgmt, Transportation & Logistics

briefs.summary: Council is set to consider a bylaw at its April 28, 2026 meeting (Item 11.2 — First, Second and Third Reading). No body text was provided, so the specific subject, rules, or requirements of this proposed bylaw cannot be summarized. Please supply the full agenda item text for an accurate brief.
briefs.action_required: Check the Burnaby Council agenda for Item 11.2 details before the April 28, 2026 meeting.

key_dates.label: Burnaby Council meeting — Item 11.2 bylaw readings scheduled

briefs.title: Proposed: Burnaby Bylaw Would Set New Rules for E-Bikes and Electric Kick Scooters
briefs.summary: Council is set to vote on a proposed bylaw that would update Burnaby's Street and Traffic rules to formally include e-bikes (electric-assist bicycles) and electric kick scooters. The bylaw would set where these devices can ride and park, ban them from most sidewalks and major roads unless a protected bike lane or multi-use pathway is available, and create designated parking zones for shared rental devices like dockless scooters and bikes.
briefs.action_required: Watch for the council vote — if you operate or use shared e-bikes or e-scooters in Burnaby, new parking and riding rules may apply.

briefs.title: Proposed: Burnaby bylaw would regulate e-bikes and electric scooters in city parks
briefs.summary: Council is set to vote on a proposed bylaw that would update Burnaby's parks rules to officially include e-bikes (motor assisted cycles) and electric kick scooters. The change would mean these devices can only be ridden or parked in parks where the City has specifically allowed it — just like regular bikes and vehicles are already regulated.
briefs.action_required: Watch for the council decision — this could affect where you or your customers ride e-bikes or scooters near parks.

briefs.title: Proposed: Burnaby Bylaw to Add E-Bikes and Electric Scooters to Park Rules
briefs.summary: Council is set to vote on a bylaw amendment that would update Burnaby's park rules to explicitly include e-bikes (electric-assist bicycles) and electric kick scooters. A proposed bylaw would make it a ticketable offence — with a $200 fine ($160 if paid early) — to ride or park these vehicles in park areas not designated for their use.
briefs.action_required: Watch for the council vote — this could affect how you or your staff use Burnaby parks with e-bikes or scooters.

briefs.title: Proposed: Burnaby 2026 Property Tax Rates Up for Council Approval
briefs.summary: Council is set to vote on Bylaw No. 14814, which would set the property tax rates for all land and buildings in Burnaby for 2026. A proposed bylaw would also apply a special local tax (an extra levy charged to businesses in a defined area) to properties in the Hastings Business Improvement Area.
briefs.action_required: Check your property class and review the proposed mill rates — they determine your 2026 tax bill.

key_dates.label: Burnaby Rates Bylaw 2026 — council vote at meeting
Burnaby Rates Bylaw 2026 — adoption deadline under Community Charter

briefs.summary: Burnaby City Council is set to vote on scheduling a Parks, Recreation and Culture Committee (PRCC) meeting for Wednesday, May 27, 2026, at 5:00 pm in the Council Chamber. This is a procedural step — it simply sets the date and time for the committee to meet, with no direct regulatory changes proposed at this stage.
